title: Curricular Practical Training (CPT) — ISSS
content: Curricular Practical Training (CPT) is off-campus work authorization for F-1 international students at the University of Minnesota. CPT allows F-1 students to work in positions directly related to their field of study, including internships, co-ops, and practicums. You must receive ISSS authorization before starting CPT work — working without authorization is a visa violation. CPT requires enrollment in a course that requires the practical experience. For eligibility requirements, the application process, deadlines, and current policy details, visit the ISSS CPT page directly at https://isss.umn.edu/fstudents/employment/cpt or contact ISSS at isss@umn.edu.

title: Optional Practical Training (OPT) — ISSS
content: Optional Practical Training (OPT) is temporary employment authorization for F-1 international students that allows work directly related to your major area of study. Pre-completion OPT is available before graduation; post-completion OPT is available after graduation. STEM degree holders may be eligible for a 24-month STEM OPT extension. You must apply for OPT through ISSS and receive an Employment Authorization Document (EAD) from USCIS before starting work. For application deadlines, eligibility, and step-by-step instructions, visit https://isss.umn.edu/fstudents/employment/opt or contact ISSS at isss@umn.edu.

title: Reduced Course Load (RCL) — ISSS
content: F-1 international students are required to maintain full-time enrollment. A Reduced Course Load (RCL) allows an F-1 student to drop below full-time credit requirements under specific approved circumstances, such as academic difficulty in the first semester, a medical condition, or final semester with fewer credits needed. You must receive ISSS authorization before dropping below full-time — dropping without authorization is a visa violation. For eligibility criteria, required documentation, and the authorization process, visit https://isss.umn.edu/fstudent/rcl.html or contact ISSS at isss@umn.edu.

title: Graduate Assistant Employment — UMN Human Resources
content: Graduate assistantships at the University of Minnesota include Teaching Assistantships (TA), Research Assistantships (RA), and Graduate School Fellowships. Graduate assistants typically receive a tuition benefit, stipend, and subsidized health insurance. Appointment levels (25%, 50%, 75%) determine tuition coverage and stipend amounts. Eligibility, appointment terms, and benefit details are governed by HR policy. For current pay rates, benefit summaries, and employment terms, visit https://hr.umn.edu/Jobs/Student-Job-Center/Graduate-Assistant-Employment/About-Graduate-Assistant-Employment or contact the CS graduate program at csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: Graduate and Professional Student Assembly (GAPSA) — formerly GSC
content: The Graduate and Professional Student Assembly (GAPSA) represents graduate and professional students at the University of Minnesota. GAPSA provides health insurance through the Graduate Student Health Plan, manages the Emergency Loan Fund, and advocates for graduate student interests. Graduate students can access mental health resources, legal services, and community programs through GAPSA. For health plan enrollment, emergency funding, and student advocacy resources, visit https://gsc.umn.edu/ or contact GAPSA directly.

title: Student Academic Support Services (SASS)
content: Student Academic Support Services (SASS) at the University of Minnesota provides academic coaching, writing support, and learning strategy resources for graduate students. Services include one-on-one academic skills coaching, workshops on topics like time management and exam preparation, and self-help resources for graduate students. SASS is available to all enrolled graduate students. For appointment scheduling and available services, visit https://sass.umn.edu/ or contact SASS directly.

title: Master's Time Extension Request Form (UM1779)
content: The Master's Time Extension Request Form (UM1779) is used when an M.S. student needs to extend the standard time limit for degree completion beyond the allowed period. Time extensions require approval from the graduate program and the Graduate School. This form must be submitted before the degree deadline expires. The form was previously available at https://onestop.umn.edu/sites/onestop.umn.edu/files/forms/um1779_masters_time_extension_request_form.pdf — if the link is unavailable, contact the CS graduate program coordinators at csgradmn@umn.edu or visit OneStop at onestop.umn.edu for the current form.

title: Doctoral Time Extension Request Form (UM1777)
content: The Doctoral Time Extension Request Form (UM1777) is used when a Ph.D. student needs to extend the standard time limit for degree completion. Doctoral students typically have 8 years to complete the degree. Extensions require graduate program and Graduate School approval. If the PDF link is unavailable, contact the CS graduate program coordinators at csgradmn@umn.edu or visit OneStop at onestop.umn.edu to locate the current version.

title: Graduate Student Petition Form
content: The Graduate Student Petition Form is used to request exceptions to graduate program policies, such as course substitutions, credit overloads, late withdrawals, or waiver of specific requirements. Petitions must be approved by the graduate program and may require Graduate School review depending on the nature of the request. If the PDF is unavailable, contact the CS graduate program coordinators at csgradmn@umn.edu or visit OneStop at onestop.umn.edu for the current petition form.

title: CS Graduate Handbook Archive
content: The CS Graduate Handbook Archive contains past versions of the UMN CS Graduate Handbook. Students are typically held to the requirements in effect at the time they entered the program. If you need to reference an older version of the handbook for your specific cohort requirements, visit https://www.cs.umn.edu/academics/graduate/grad-handbook-archive or contact csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: CS Graduate TA Academic Policy
content: The UMN CS department TA Academic Policy outlines academic performance standards required to hold a Teaching Assistantship. TAs must maintain satisfactory academic standing and meet GPA requirements. Violations of the policy can result in loss of the TA appointment. For the full policy text, visit https://cse.umn.edu/cs/graduate-ta-academic-policy or contact csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: CS Graduate Academic Policy
content: The UMN CS department academic policy page covers graduate program academic standards including satisfactory progress requirements, GPA thresholds, and policies governing academic standing. For the full current policy, visit https://cse.umn.edu/cs/academic-policy or contact the CS graduate program at csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: Graduate School Funding — University of Minnesota
content: The UMN Graduate School funding page lists funding opportunities for graduate students including fellowships, grants, and awards administered through the Graduate School. This includes the Doctoral Dissertation Fellowship (DDF), diversity fellowships, and travel grants. For current opportunities, deadlines, and eligibility, visit https://grad.umn.edu/funding or contact your program's graduate coordinator.

title: Doctoral Dissertation Fellowship (DDF) — Graduate School
content: The Doctoral Dissertation Fellowship (DDF) is a competitive University of Minnesota Graduate School fellowship for doctoral students who are close to completing their dissertation. DDF recipients receive a stipend, tuition, and subsidized health insurance for one year. Students must be nominated by their department. For eligibility criteria, nomination process, and current deadlines, visit https://grad.umn.edu/funding/current-students/apply-through-program/doctoral-dissertation-fellowship or contact csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: CS TA Handbook — University of Minnesota
content: The UMN CS TA Handbook covers responsibilities, expectations, and policies for Teaching Assistants in the CS department. It includes information on course assignments, grading responsibilities, office hours, and professional conduct. For the current handbook, visit https://www.cs.umn.edu/academics/graduate/ta_handbook or contact csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: Graduate School Readmission — University of Minnesota
content: Students who have discontinued their graduate studies at UMN and wish to return must apply for readmission through the Graduate School. Readmission is not guaranteed and depends on program availability and academic standing at the time of discontinuation. For the readmission process and required materials, visit https://grad.umn.edu/admissions/readmission or contact the CS graduate program at csgradmn@umn.edu.

title: UMN Academic Calendar — OneStop
content: The UMN Academic Calendar lists all official university dates including semester start and end dates, registration periods, final exam schedules, holidays, and degree application deadlines. Graduate students should check the calendar for add/drop deadlines, tuition due dates, and graduation application deadlines. The calendar is updated each year. For current dates visit https://onestop.umn.edu/calendar/academic-calendar or contact OneStop at onestop.umn.edu.

title: CS Career Services — UMN Computer Science
content: The UMN CS department career services page provides resources for CS students including job search support, resume reviews, interview preparation, and connections to employers. CS career events and recruiting information are available through this page. For current resources and upcoming career events visit https://cse.umn.edu/cs/career-services or contact the CS department directly.

title: CS Graduate Program Faculty Responsibilities — UMN
content: This page outlines faculty responsibilities within the UMN CS graduate program including advising, committee service, and program governance. For details visit https://faculty-roles.umn.edu/institution/UMNTC/programs/019660207/responsibilities or contact csgradmn@umn.edu.
